title: Fraud Examination Techniques
description: Master advanced fraud detection and examination techniques for internal auditors

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Fraud costs organizations an estimated 5% of revenue annually (ACFE Report to the Nations). Internal auditors play a crucial role in fraud detection and deterrence. Understanding examination techniques is essential for CIA Part 2.

sections.title: Fraud Detection Methods
sections.content:
**Internal auditors use multiple methods to detect fraud:**

**1. Data Analytics**
• Benford's Law analysis (digit frequency analysis)
• Duplicate payment testing
• Gaps and duplicates in sequential numbering
• Stratification analysis (identifying outliers)
• Relative size factor testing

**2. Accounting Analysis**
• Horizontal analysis (trend comparison)
• Vertical analysis (common-size statements)
• Ratio analysis (unusual fluctuations)
• Journal entry testing (unusual entries)
• Account reconciliation review

**3. Document Examination**
• Alteration detection
• Signature analysis
• Photocopies vs. originals
• Supporting documentation verification
• Vendor file review

sections.title: Benford's Law Application
sections.content:
**Benford's Law** states that in naturally occurring datasets, the first digit follows a predictable distribution:

• **Digit 1**: ~30.1% frequency
• **Digit 2**: ~17.6% frequency
• **Digit 3**: ~12.5% frequency
• **Digit 4**: ~9.7% frequency
• **Digits 5-9**: Decreasing percentages

**Application in Fraud Detection:**
• Compare actual first-digit distribution to expected
• Significant deviations indicate potential manipulation
• Works best on large datasets (invoices, payments)
• NOT applicable to assigned numbers (SSN, account numbers)
• Particularly useful for expense reports, reimbursements

sections.title: Red Flag Categories
rows: Living beyond means, financial difficulties, refusal to take vacation
Missing documents, altered records, excessive voids
Unusually close vendor relationships, lifestyle changes
Single-source contracts, inflated invoices, kickback patterns
Pressure to meet targets, resistance to audit, management override
Unusual journal entries, revenue timing issues, reserve manipulation

sections.title: Fraud Interview Techniques
sections.content:
**Interview Progression:**

**1. Information-Gathering Interview**
• Non-accusatory tone
• Open-ended questions
• Establish baseline behavior
• Gather facts and timeline

**2. Assessment Interview**
• Observe verbal and non-verbal cues
• Identify inconsistencies
• Test alibis and explanations

**3. Admission-Seeking Interview (if warranted)**
• Only after sufficient evidence gathered
• Follow legal and HR guidelines
• Document carefully
• Consider legal counsel involvement

**Key Principles:**
• Maintain objectivity
• Document everything
• Protect whistleblower identity
• Follow organization's investigation policy

sections.title: Memory Aid: MICE
sections.content:
**Fraud Motivators (MICE):**

**M**oney - Financial pressure or greed
**I**deology - Belief they are entitled or justified
**C**oercion - External pressure or threats
**E**go - Status, recognition, power

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Benford's Law analyzes first-digit distribution to detect manipulation
Data analytics can test 100% of transactions for anomalies
Red flags are categorized by fraud type (misappropriation, corruption, financial statement)
Interview progression: information gathering → assessment → admission seeking
Always follow organizational policies and involve legal/HR as appropriate

knowledgeCheck.question: An internal auditor is analyzing a dataset of expense reports using Benford's Law. They observe that the digit '1' appears as the leading digit in only 15% of the entries. Based on this information, which of the following conclusions is MOST appropriate?
options: The dataset conforms to Benford's Law, indicating no significant anomalies.
The dataset likely contains errors or fraudulent activity, warranting further investigation.
The dataset is too small to apply Benford's Law effectively.
Benford's Law is not applicable to expense report data.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: Benford's Law predicts that the digit '1' should appear as the leading digit approximately 30.1% of the time. A significant deviation from this percentage (such as 15%) suggests potential anomalies, errors, or fraudulent activity that warrants further investigation. While sample size can impact the accuracy of Benford's Law, a deviation as large as this is still a strong indicator of a problem. Expense reports are generally a suitable data type for Benford's Law analysis.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider the expected frequency of the digit '1' according to Benford's Law.

reveal.question: What is the PRIMARY goal of an information-gathering interview in a fraud examination?
reveal.answer: The primary goal is to gather facts and establish a timeline in a non-accusatory manner, establishing baseline behavior of the interviewee. This stage focuses on obtaining information without raising suspicion or prematurely accusing anyone.

rows.label: Tone
values: Potentially more direct, focusing on inconsistencies

title: Statistical Sampling Formulas and Techniques
description: Master the mathematical foundations of statistical sampling for audit testing

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Statistical sampling allows auditors to make objective, defensible conclusions about a population based on a sample. Understanding the formulas helps you properly design samples and interpret results. The CIA exam tests both concepts and calculations.

sections.title: Key Sampling Concepts
sections.content:
**Confidence Level (Reliability)**
• Probability that sample result reflects population
• Common levels: 90%, 95%, 99%
• Higher confidence = larger sample required
• 95% confidence means 5% risk of wrong conclusion

**Precision (Tolerable Error)**
• Acceptable range of deviation from sample result
• Also called 'allowable error' or 'precision'
• Tighter precision = larger sample required

**Expected Error Rate**
• Anticipated deviation rate in population
• Based on prior experience or pilot testing
• Higher expected rate = larger sample required

**Population Size (N)**
• Total items in population
• Less impact on sample size than other factors
• Finite population correction applies when sampling >5% of population

sections.title: Attribute Sampling Formula
sections.content:
**Attribute Sampling** tests yes/no characteristics (compliance testing)

**Sample Size Formula:**
```
n = (Z² × p × (1-p)) / E²
```

Where:
• **n** = Sample size
• **Z** = Z-score for confidence level (1.65 for 90%, 1.96 for 95%, 2.58 for 99%)
• **p** = Expected error rate (as decimal)
• **E** = Precision/tolerable error rate (as decimal)

**Example:**
• Confidence: 95% (Z = 1.96)
• Expected error: 2% (p = 0.02)
• Tolerable error: 5% (E = 0.05)

n = (1.96² × 0.02 × 0.98) / 0.05²
n = (3.84 × 0.0196) / 0.0025
n = 0.0753 / 0.0025
n = **30 items**

sections.title: Variables Sampling Approaches
sections.content:
**Variables Sampling** tests dollar amounts (substantive testing)

**1. Mean-Per-Unit (MPU)**
• Estimates population total from sample mean
• Population estimate = Sample mean × Population size
• Standard formula but high variability

**2. Difference Estimation**
• Estimates total error from sample differences
• Audited value - Book value for each item
• More efficient when errors are expected

**3. Ratio Estimation**
• Uses ratio of audited to book values
• Population estimate = Book total × (Sample audited / Sample book)
• Efficient when proportional relationship exists

**4. Monetary Unit Sampling (MUS/PPS)**
• Each dollar is a sampling unit
• Larger items more likely to be selected
• Combines attributes and variables approaches
• Most commonly used for substantive testing

sections.title: Sample Size Relationships
sections.content:
**Factors that INCREASE sample size:**
• Higher confidence level desired
• Lower tolerable error (tighter precision)
• Higher expected error rate
• Greater population variability

**Factors that DECREASE sample size:**
• Lower confidence level acceptable
• Higher tolerable error
• Lower expected error rate
• Smaller population (for finite correction)

sections.title: Evaluating Sample Results
sections.content:
**Calculating Upper Deviation Rate:**

After testing, compute the upper deviation rate:

**Upper Limit = Sample Rate + Precision Adjustment**

**Decision Rule:**
• If Upper Limit < Tolerable Rate → Accept (control is effective)
• If Upper Limit ≥ Tolerable Rate → Reject (control may not be effective)

**Example:**
• Tolerable rate: 5%
• Sample: 100 items, 3 exceptions
• Sample rate: 3%
• Precision adjustment: 1.5% (from statistical tables)
• Upper limit: 3% + 1.5% = 4.5%
• Since 4.5% < 5%, conclude control is effective at stated confidence

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Confidence level, precision, and expected error are the key drivers of sample size
Attribute sampling tests compliance (yes/no); Variables sampling tests amounts
MUS/PPS is most commonly used for substantive testing
Compare upper deviation limit to tolerable rate to evaluate results

knowledgeCheck.question: An internal auditor is performing attribute sampling to test the effectiveness of a company's internal controls. The auditor decides to increase the confidence level from 95% to 99%. All other factors remain constant. What is the MOST likely effect on the required sample size?
options: The sample size will remain the same.
The effect on sample size is indeterminate.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: A higher confidence level requires a larger sample size. This is because a higher confidence level means the auditor wants to be more certain that the sample results accurately reflect the population, which necessitates examining more items.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Think about the relationship between risk and sample size. Higher confidence reduces risk.

reveal.question: State the formula for determining sample size in attribute sampling.
reveal.answer:
n = (Z² × p × (1-p)) / E²  Where:
n = Sample size
Z = Z-score for confidence level
p = Expected error rate (as decimal)
E = Tolerable error (precision) as a decimal

sections.title: Variables Sampling: MPU vs. Difference Estimation

rows.label: Primary Focus
values: Estimates the total population value directly from the sample mean.
Estimates the total error amount by calculating the average difference between audited values and book values within the sample.

rows.label: Formula Core
values: Population estimate = Sample mean × Population size
Focus on differences between audited and book values to project total error.

rows.label: Variability Impact
values: Highly susceptible to variability within the population, potentially leading to less precise estimates.
More effective when the errors are relatively consistent across the population, reducing the impact of variability.

title: IFRS vs US GAAP: Key Differences for Auditors
description: Understand the critical differences between IFRS and US GAAP that impact internal audit

sections.title: Why This Matters
sections.content: Internal auditors in global organizations must understand both IFRS and US GAAP. The CIA exam tests knowledge of key differences. Over 140 countries use IFRS, while US companies follow US GAAP. Many multinationals must reconcile between both frameworks.

sections.title: Framework Comparison
sections.content:
**IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards)**
• Issued by: International Accounting Standards Board (IASB)
• Approach: Principles-based
• Used by: 140+ countries worldwide
• Focus: Fair value and economic substance

**US GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles)**
• Issued by: Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB)
• Approach: Rules-based
• Used by: United States (SEC registrants required)
• Focus: Historical cost and detailed guidance

**Key Philosophical Differences:**
• IFRS emphasizes professional judgment
• US GAAP provides more specific rules and bright-line tests
• IFRS uses more fair value measurements
• US GAAP has more industry-specific guidance

sections.title: Major Differences: Revenue Recognition
sections.content:
**Revenue Recognition (ASC 606 / IFRS 15)**

Both frameworks now use the 5-step model, but differences remain:

**1. Contract Modifications**
• IFRS: More general guidance
• GAAP: More specific prospective vs. cumulative treatment

**2. Right of Return**
• IFRS: More judgment in estimating returns
• GAAP: More prescriptive rules

**3. Licenses**
• Some differences in point-in-time vs. over-time recognition

**5-Step Model (Both):**
1. Identify contract with customer
2. Identify performance obligations
3. Determine transaction price
4. Allocate price to obligations
5. Recognize revenue when/as obligations satisfied

sections.title: Major Differences: Financial Instruments
sections.content:
**Classification:**
• IFRS (IFRS 9): Three categories based on business model and cash flow characteristics
  - Amortized cost
  - FVOCI (Fair Value through Other Comprehensive Income)
  - FVPL (Fair Value through Profit/Loss)

• GAAP (ASC 320/321): More categories
  - Trading securities
  - Available-for-sale
  - Held-to-maturity
  - Equity method investments

**Impairment:**
• IFRS: Expected credit loss model (forward-looking)
• GAAP: Current expected credit loss (CECL) model - similar approach

**Derivatives:**
• Both require fair value measurement
• Hedge accounting rules differ in application

sections.title: Memory Aid: Key IFRS-Only Rules
sections.content:
**IFRS Allows What GAAP Prohibits (RRIL):**

**R**evaluation of fixed assets upward
**R**eversal of inventory write-downs
**I**FRS = no LIFO
**L**ast-in-first-out is prohibited

sections.title: Convergence Status
sections.content:
**Areas of Convergence:**
• Revenue recognition (ASC 606 / IFRS 15)
• Leases (ASC 842 / IFRS 16)
• Credit losses (CECL / ECL models)

**Areas Still Divergent:**
• Inventory (LIFO, write-down reversal)
• Fixed asset revaluation
• Research and development
• Statement presentation and terminology

**Practical Implications for Auditors:**
• Verify correct framework applied
• Review reconciliations for dual reporters
• Understand management judgments under IFRS
• Check consistency of accounting policies

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: IFRS is principles-based; US GAAP is rules-based
LIFO is prohibited under IFRS but permitted under US GAAP
IFRS allows/requires reversal of inventory write-downs; US GAAP prohibits
IFRS permits upward revaluation of fixed assets; US GAAP does not
IFRS requires capitalizing development costs meeting criteria; US GAAP expenses most R&D

knowledgeCheck.question: Which of the following statements BEST describes the primary difference between IFRS and US GAAP in terms of their approach to financial reporting?
options: IFRS is primarily rules-based, providing detailed guidance for specific transactions, while US GAAP is principles-based, relying on broad concepts and professional judgment.
IFRS is primarily principles-based, emphasizing the economic substance of transactions and professional judgment, while US GAAP is rules-based, providing detailed guidance for specific transactions.
Both IFRS and US GAAP are equally rules-based, but they differ in the specific rules they apply.
Both IFRS and US GAAP are equally principles-based, but they differ in the interpretation of those principles.
knowledgeCheck.explanation: IFRS emphasizes principles, allowing for professional judgment to reflect the economic substance of transactions. US GAAP relies on detailed rules to ensure consistency and comparability.
knowledgeCheck.hint: Consider which framework provides more specific, detailed instructions and which relies more on broad concepts.

reveal.question: List the five steps of the revenue recognition model used in both IFRS 15 and ASC 606.
reveal.answer: 1. Identify the contract(s) with a customer. 2. Identify the performance obligations in the contract. 3. Determine the transaction price. 4. Allocate the transaction price to the performance obligations in the contract. 5. Recognize revenue when (or as) the entity satisfies a performance obligation.

rows.label: Contract Modifications Guidance
values: Provides more general guidance, relying on professional judgment.
Provides more specific guidance on prospective vs. cumulative catch-up treatment.

rows.label: Level of Detail
values: Lower level of detail; requires more interpretation.
Higher level of detail; provides more prescriptive rules.
